title: Kleftiko
subtitle: Greek paper-wrapped slow-cooked lamb
excerpt: Lamb shoulder joints sealed in greaseproof paper parcels with lemon, garlic, and oregano, then slow-roasted until the meat falls from the bone. A Greek village classic that looks dramatic and asks very little of the cook. Serves four.
sourceNotes: Kleftiko is a traditional Greek dish from the mainland and islands, associated with the klephts, mountain bandits who slow-cooked stolen lamb in sealed earth pits to avoid detection. The modern oven version is documented by Tessa Kiros in Falling Cloudberries (2004) and by Claudia Roden in The Food of the Eastern Mediterranean.

content.text: Kleftiko is one of the simplest slow-roasts in the Greek repertoire. The lamb goes into a parcel of greaseproof paper with lemon, garlic, potatoes, and oregano, the parcel gets sealed tightly, and the oven does three and a half hours of quiet work. When you open the parcel at the table, the steam carries the whole smell of the dish in one hit.

Lamb shoulder is the right cut. It has the fat and connective tissue that a long slow cook needs. Leg of lamb dries out. Bone-in pieces give more flavour than boneless, though boneless shoulder works if that is what is available. The potatoes cook inside the parcel and absorb the lamb juices, which is the real prize of the dish.

items.name: Lamb shoulder
items.prepNote: bone-in, cut into 4 large pieces by the butcher, or two pieces from a half shoulder

items.name: Potato
items.prepNote: peeled and cut into thick wedges

items.name: Feta
items.prepNote: crumbled, to scatter inside the parcels

content.text: You need a large roasting tin and enough greaseproof paper and kitchen twine to seal the parcels securely. Cut the paper generously, each piece should be large enough to fold over the lamb and potatoes with plenty of overlap at the top.

Mix {{olive-oil}} of olive oil with the lemon juice, {{oregano-dried}} of dried oregano, {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, and {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Score the lamb pieces deeply with a small knife and push a halved clove of garlic into each cut. Rub the marinade over all surfaces of the lamb. Cover and refrigerate for at least 1 hour, or overnight.

Heat the oven to 160°C fan. Lay a large sheet of greaseproof paper in the roasting tin. Place a portion of potatoes on the paper, lay a piece of lamb on top, tuck in a few garlic halves and a slice of lemon rind, and scatter with feta if using. Bring the paper up and over, folding it tightly at the top and sides like a parcel, then tie securely with kitchen twine. Repeat for each portion. The seals must be tight, any gaps will lose the steam that does the cooking.

Roast for 3 hours 30 minutes without opening the parcels. The lamb is done when the juices inside have reduced to a concentrated, fragrant pool and the meat pulls easily from the bone. Tear open the parcels at the table (the steam will be very hot) and serve directly from the paper with the cooking juices poured over.

items.fix: Re-seal and return for another 30 to 45 minutes. Shoulder needs sustained steam to break down its connective tissue.
items.cause: Parcel not sealed tightly enough, or oven temperature too low.

items.fix: Potatoes should be in contact with the base of the parcel where the juices pool. Wedges no thicker than 2 cm cook reliably in 3 hours.
items.cause: Potatoes cut too thick, or placed on top of the lamb rather than underneath.

items.fix: Use double-thickness greaseproof paper, or line with a sheet of foil on the outside of the paper parcel as a reinforcing layer.
items.cause: Paper too thin or creased too sharply at the folds.
items.symptom: Parcels steaming in the oven but tearing

items.fix: An overnight marinade is noticeably better than one hour. The garlic inserted into the slits flavours the meat from the inside, do not skip this step.

content.text: The name kleftiko comes from the Greek word for thief. According to the story, the mountain bandits who resisted Ottoman rule in the 18th and 19th centuries would steal a sheep and cook it in a sealed underground pit so that neither smoke nor smell escaped to give them away. The modern version with its greaseproof paper parcels in a domestic oven captures the logic of the original: sealing everything in together so nothing evaporates and the lamb slowly braises in its own steam and juices. It is a dish that rewards patience and very little else.
